The administration’s insistence on "peace through strength" is rooted in the volatile events of the past year. Following the 12-day conflict in June 2025, during which U.S. and Israeli forces conducted surgical strikes against Iranian nuclear infrastructure, Washington has hardened its position to a "zero enrichment" policy to prevent the regime from rebuilding its capabilities. Coupled with recent, deadly internal unrest that has shaken the clerical regime's domestic stability, the White House calculates that this ticking clock, combined with visible firepower, offers the only viable path to a definitive resolution. However, as the 10-day countdown begins, the risk of miscalculation by either side remains acute.

title: Artemis II: NASA High-Stakes Fueling Test Underway to Secure March Launch
description: KENNEDY SPACE CENTER, Fla.
content: KENNEDY SPACE CENTER, Fla. — NASA is currently executing a high-stakes, second "wet dress rehearsal" for the Artemis II mission, a critical fueling test that will determine when the first crewed flight to the moon in over 50 years can finally get off the ground. As of Thursday, the countdown clock at Launch Pad 39B is ticking toward a simulated launch time of 8:30 p.m. EST. The massive Space Launch System (SLS) rocket is in its tanking phase, a highly complex process of loading more than 700,000 gallons of super-cold liquid oxygen and liquid hydrogen into the rocket’s core and upper stages. Launch controllers are meticulously running through terminal countdown procedures, simulating the exact conditions, holds, and recycles they will face on launch day. Operators are tasked with conducting two separate runs of the final ten minutes of the countdown. The sequence involves pausing at T-minus 1 minute and 30 seconds, resuming, and pausing again at T-minus 33 seconds. Following this, the clock will recycle back to T-minus 10 minutes for a second simulated run. This rigorous process tests the team's ability to handle technical or weather-related launch scrub scenarios in real-time. Overcoming Hydrogen Leaks and Hardware Hurdles This second rehearsal was mandated after a series of frustrating but necessary delays. The initial wet dress rehearsal on February 3 was terminated at T-minus 5 minutes and 15 seconds due to a liquid hydrogen leak at an umbilical interface. A subsequent partial fueling test on February 12 uncovered a separate issue: a clogged filter in the ground support equipment that was restricting the flow of liquid hydrogen into the core stage. Engineers successfully replaced the faulty filter over the weekend, clearing the way for today's full-scale simulation. While the four-person Artemis II crew—NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen—are not aboard the Orion capsule for this test, ground teams are treating the pad as if they are. A specialized "closeout crew" was dispatched to practice the exact procedures for securing the astronauts and sealing the Orion spacecraft’s hatch, ensuring the environment is perfectly safe for human occupants. Looking Ahead to a March Launch Artemis II is slated to send its crew on a 10-day lunar flyby, testing Orion's deep-space life support and navigation systems ahead of the Artemis III lunar landing. If today's tanking and terminal countdown operations conclude successfully, NASA will clear the final major ground hurdle and set an official launch date. Agency managers have identified March 6, 2026, as the earliest possible launch window, which allows sufficient time for post-test data review and the transition to actual launch operations. NASA leadership will hold a press conference tomorrow, February 20, at 11:00 a.m. ET to share the official results of the wet dress rehearsal and outline the final path to the launchpad.

title: From Home Invasion to Homicide: Husband Charged in Murder of Tipp City Teacher Ashley Flynn
description: TIPP CITY, Ohio — A devastating plot twist has rocked the close-knit community of Tipp City.
content: TIPP CITY, Ohio — A devastating plot twist has rocked the close-knit community of Tipp City. Caleb Carl Flynn, 39, the husband of beloved local teacher and volleyball coach Ashley Flynn, has been arrested and charged with her murder following a rigorous, days-long investigation that initially began as a reported home invasion. Caleb Flynn was taken into custody and booked into the Miami County Jail at approximately 5:07 p.m. on Thursday, Feb. 19. Tipp City Police Chief Greg Adkins confirmed that Flynn faces a severe slate of charges, including one count of murder, two counts of felonious assault, and two counts of tampering with evidence. The tampering charges stem from investigators finding probable cause that the early Monday morning "break-in" on Cunningham Court was staged. The tragic sequence of events began around 2:31 a.m. on Feb. 16, when Caleb Flynn called 911. According to dispatch logs, he reported that an intruder had broken into their home, noting an open garage door and stating his wife had been shot in the head. During the frantic call, he initially claimed he and his daughter were locked in a bedroom, before correcting himself to say the couple's two juvenile daughters were asleep in their own rooms. First responders arrived to find 37-year-old Ashley Flynn suffering from two fatal gunshot wounds. She was pronounced dead at the scene, while the children were found physically unharmed. The arrest has shocked neighbors and churchgoers who knew the family well. Caleb Flynn is a former music pastor who previously served on staff at the Christian Life Center in Butler Township. He also gained local notoriety in 2013 as a contestant on Season 12 of "American Idol," where he publicly professed his deep love for his wife during a hometown interview segment. Chief Adkins emphasized the complexity of the case, praising the "tireless, around-the-clock" efforts of local detectives, the Miami County Sheriff’s Office, the Ohio Bureau of Criminal Investigation (BCI), and the FBI. "Their work is not complete, and they will continue to pursue this case with integrity and dedication as it proceeds through the court process," Adkins stated, reassuring the public that this was an isolated incident with no ongoing threat to the community. As the legal process begins, Tipp City continues to mourn the loss of Ashley Flynn. Remembered for her warm smile and genuine care, she was a 7th-grade volleyball coach at Tippecanoe Middle School, a dedicated substitute teacher, and an educator at Lifewise Academy. The community has rallied fiercely, raising over $80,000 through an online fundraiser and tying memorial ribbons around downtown Tipp City. Local businesses are even donating portions of their profits to support the family. A celebration of life for Ashley Flynn is scheduled for Sunday, Feb. 22, at 4:00 p.m. at the Christian Life Center.

title: Amazon's Liability Shock: When Algorithms Cross the Line
description: OLYMPIA, Wash. — The Washington State Supreme Court delivered a unanimous ruling on February 19, 2026, that will undoubtedly send ripples through the e-commerce sector.
content: OLYMPIA, Wash. — The Washington State Supreme Court delivered a unanimous ruling on February 19, 2026, that will undoubtedly send ripples through the e-commerce sector. The court decided that families of individuals who tragically took their own lives using highly concentrated sodium nitrite purchased on Amazon can proceed to trial, reversing a lower court's dismissal. At first glance, this ruling might trigger alarms for those who champion free markets and individual responsibility. Traditionally, the law provides a robust "misuse defense": a retailer cannot be held liable if a consumer intentionally uses a legal product to harm themselves. Holding a platform accountable for the tragic choices of its users threatens to set a slippery precedent. However, the justices didn't discard personal responsibility; rather, they focused heavily on the unique intersection of product purity and algorithmic behavior. The chemical at the center of the dispute, sodium nitrite, is entirely legal and commonly used at very low concentrations (around 6%) to cure meats. The controversy stems from the specific product sold: reagent-grade sodium nitrite at 98% to 99.6% purity. At this concentration, the chemical has virtually no legitimate household use and is highly lethal. What transforms this from a standard product liability case into a landmark tech-accountability issue is the role of Amazon's proprietary algorithms. The plaintiffs allege that Amazon's "Frequently Bought Together" feature actively bundled the deadly chemical with anti-emetic drugs (to prevent the body's natural urge to vomit) and a well-known suicide instruction manual. To the court, this crossed the line from operating a passive, neutral marketplace into actively assembling a lethal toolkit for vulnerable buyers. Furthermore, the lawsuit claims Amazon was repeatedly warned by parents and regulators starting in 2018 about the fatal misuse of the product, yet the company allegedly removed one-star reviews warning of the danger while keeping the product listings active. The court found that when a retailer has explicit, repeated knowledge that a highly lethal product with no household utility is being used almost exclusively for self-harm, a legal "duty of care" emerges to prevent foreseeable tragedies. This legal shift mirrors recent legislative actions. Last year, Washington lawmakers passed "Tyler's Law" with overwhelming bipartisan support, restricting the sale of sodium nitrite in concentrations over 10% to verified businesses and laboratories. This bipartisan consensus underscores that regulating industrial-grade poisons sold to residential addresses is increasingly viewed not as government overreach, but as a necessary guardrail for public safety. For investors and e-commerce giants, the message is clear: the shield of the "neutral platform" is cracking. When algorithms actively bundle dangerous products and corporate infrastructure ignores direct warnings of localized harm, the courts—and the market—will demand accountability. Amazon has since voluntarily restricted high-concentration sodium nitrite sales to verified business accounts, a quiet acknowledgment that the era of blind algorithmic retail may be coming to an end.

title: Former Prince Andrew Released Under Investigation in Historic State Secrets Probe
description: In a historic and rapidly evolving legal drama, Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor has been released under investigation after spending nearly 12 hours in police custody.
content: In a historic and rapidly evolving legal drama, Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor has been released under investigation after spending nearly 12 hours in police custody. The former Duke of York was arrested on Thursday morning in a move legal scholars are calling unprecedented in centuries, shifting the long-running Epstein saga from civil disputes to a high-stakes criminal probe into state secrets. Detectives from Thames Valley Police executed the arrest at his Wood Farm residence on the Sandringham Estate at approximately 8:00 AM GMT, coinciding with his 66th birthday. Mountbatten-Windsor was held on suspicion of misconduct in public office. Following intense questioning at Aylsham Police Station, he was officially "Released Under Investigation" (RUI). Under U.K. law, this status means he has neither been charged nor cleared; he remains a primary suspect while authorities continue their inquiries. While police have concluded their initial search of Wood Farm, extensive searches are reportedly still underway at his former long-term residence, Royal Lodge in Berkshire. The catalyst for this extraordinary arrest is the January 30, 2026, unsealing of a massive document cache by the U.S. Justice Department. These newly public "Epstein files" contain highly specific, contemporaneous digital evidence from Mountbatten-Windsor's tenure as the U.K.'s Special Representative for International Trade and Investment (2001-2011). Investigators are heavily focused on what they term the "Five-Minute Forwards." Metadata allegedly shows the former prince receiving official government visit reports regarding trade missions to Singapore, Vietnam, and Hong Kong, and forwarding them to Jeffrey Epstein mere minutes later. Perhaps the most damaging piece of evidence is the "Helmand Province Brief." Documents appear to show Mountbatten-Windsor sending Epstein highly sensitive commercial intelligence regarding gold and uranium opportunities in war-torn Afghanistan, actively exploiting his government access while British troops were deployed in the region. Furthermore, the DOJ dump revealed 2001 emails sent to Ghislaine Maxwell from an alias account dubbed "The Invisible Man." Sent from the Royal Family's Balmoral estate, the emails allegedly show him asking Maxwell to find him "new inappropriate friends." The international and human fallout from the arrest has been immediate and profound. Following the tragic suicide of Epstein survivor Virginia Giuffre in April 2025, her family released a poignant statement celebrating the arrest, declaring: "For survivors everywhere, Virginia did this for you." The political sphere has also been rocked by the developments. U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer emphasized that "nobody is above the law," while U.S. President Donald Trump characterized the situation as "very sad" for the monarchy. Meanwhile, U.S. Attorney General Pam Bondi recently testified before Congress regarding the explosive DOJ files. The scandal is also expanding to cast a shadow over other prominent political figures, including former Labour politician Peter Mandelson, as investigators probe whether a wider network of elites shared sensitive state information with Epstein. As it stands, misconduct in public office remains one of the most serious common law offenses in the U.K., carrying a maximum penalty of life imprisonment. While Mountbatten-Windsor has returned to his temporary residence, the constitutional and legal ramifications of this unprecedented Royal arrest are only just beginning to unfold.

title: Trap Avoided: Raiders Sink IU Indy 85-73, Brace for RMU Showdown
description: FAIRBORN, Ohio — The Wright State Raiders took care of business on Thursday night, avoiding a late-season stumble by putting away a pesky IU Indy squad 85-73 at the Nutter Center.
content: FAIRBORN, Ohio — The Wright State Raiders took care of business on Thursday night, avoiding a late-season stumble by putting away a pesky IU Indy squad 85-73 at the Nutter Center. With the win, the Raiders (18-10, 13-4 Horizon League) maintain their grip on first place in the conference, ensuring the road to the tournament title still runs through Fairborn. Overcoming the Trap Game While the 12-point margin of victory looks comfortable on paper, the Jaguars (7-22, 3-15) made the Raiders earn every bit of it. IU Indy arrived in Dayton playing desperate basketball, having lost their previous three games by a combined 12 points. Wright State held a slim 40-36 advantage at halftime before making critical adjustments in the locker room. The Raiders' veteran leadership and superior depth ultimately wore down the Jaguars in the second half, allowing the home team to stretch the lead and put the game on ice. It was a mature win for a Wright State squad that knew it could not afford to look ahead. The Ultimate Test Awaits: Robert Morris With IU Indy in the rearview mirror, all eyes now turn to Sunday afternoon. The Nutter Center will be the epicenter of Horizon League basketball when the Robert Morris Colonials (18-10, 10-7) come to town for a 2 p.m. tipoff that will be broadcast nationally on ESPNU. This is not just another conference game; it is a revenge match with massive postseason implications. The Colonials are arguably the hottest team in the league right now. They arrive in Dayton riding a four-game winning streak, highlighted by an absolute 93-69 dismantling of second-place Oakland last weekend. Robert Morris boasts a balanced attack, featuring the league's fourth-best offense and third-best defense. They already have a victory over Wright State this season, having defeated the Raiders 72-66 back on February 4 at the UPMC Events Center in Pennsylvania. What to Watch on Sunday The stakes could not be higher. Wright State needs a victory to protect their precious lead over Oakland for the No. 1 overall seed, which guarantees home-court advantage throughout the Horizon League tournament. Sunday's matchup will be a battle of wills. Can Wright State's high-powered offense crack a stingy Robert Morris defense that just suffocated Oakland? Can the Raiders flip the script from their February loss and protect their home floor? If Thursday night was about surviving a trap, Sunday afternoon is about proving championship mettle. Fans need to pack the Nutter Center and bring the noise, because February basketball does not get any better than this.

title: Gem City Solar: Dayton’s $42 Million Power Play Enters Final Countdown
description: Dayton is officially moving from the huddle to the line of scrimmage on its most ambitious energy project to date.
content: Dayton is officially moving from the huddle to the line of scrimmage on its most ambitious energy project to date. The City Commission has finalized a massive 25-year service agreement for the Gem City Solar project, a 49.9-megawatt array that marks a historic shift in how the city powers its neighborhoods. This isn't just a win for the environment; it’s a strategic fiscal move designed to shield residents from the volatile spikes of the energy market for the next quarter-century. The project, managed by developer Zelestra Energy in partnership with AEP Energy Partners and the Sustainable Ohio Public Energy Council (SOPEC), is specifically tailored for the city’s residential electric aggregation program. By locking in rates now, city officials estimate a staggering $42 million in savings for Dayton residents over the life of the contract. Construction preparation is already visible on the 600-acre site near Little Richmond Road and State Route 49 in West Dayton, with full-scale installation set to kick off in early 2027 and a 'go-live' date slated for 2028. What makes Gem City Solar particularly interesting is its intentional design. At 49.9 MW, the facility sits just a fraction below the 50 MW threshold that would classify it as a 'utility-scale' project under the Ohio Power Siting Board. This strategic sizing allows the city to maintain more local control and avoid the bureaucratic hurdles that have recently stalled similar projects in neighboring counties. Once active, it will generate enough clean electricity to power approximately 14,600 local homes—accounting for roughly 30% of the entire residential load for the city’s aggregation pool. Beyond the grid, the community benefits are widespread. The Trotwood-Madison City School District is expected to be a primary beneficiary, receiving an estimated $7 million in tax-equivalent payments over the project's lifespan. Furthermore, the agreement mandates that at least 70% of the construction workforce must be Ohio residents, ensuring that the economic impact stays within the state and supports local labor. Environmentally, the site is being treated as more than just a power plant. Drawing inspiration from the 'solar prairie' at the University of Dayton, the developers are planting native seeds and pollinator-friendly vegetation beneath the panels. This approach helps manage stormwater runoff, restores local soil health, and protects the existing wetlands on the property. For a city that has declared a climate emergency with a goal of 100% renewable energy by 2040, Gem City Solar is effectively the cornerstone of Dayton's future energy roster.

title: U.S. Women's Hockey Caps Off Historic Gold Medal Run With Overtime Victory Against Canada
description: The United States Women's National Hockey Team cemented their legacy as one of the most dominant squads in Olympic history on Thursday, capturing the gold medal with a gritty 2-1 overtime victory against archrival Canada.
content: The United States Women's National Hockey Team cemented their legacy as one of the most dominant squads in Olympic history on Thursday, capturing the gold medal with a gritty 2-1 overtime victory against archrival Canada. The Americans tore through the 2026 Milan-Cortina Games with a flawless 7-0 record, outscoring opponents by a staggering 33-2 margin to secure their first undefeated Olympic run since the inaugural 1998 tournament. Under the guidance of head coach John Wroblewski, the U.S. roster perfectly blended seasoned veterans with a new wave of breakout youth, executing a physically demanding and relentless style of play that overwhelmed the rest of the world. Despite their statistical dominance leading up to the final, the U.S. faced a massive test against a deeply familiar Canadian squad. The championship clash was a grueling defensive battle from the opening puck drop, with U.S. goaltender Aerin Frankel and Canadian netminder Ann-Renee Desbiens trading spectacular saves. Canada drew first blood just 54 seconds into the second period when Kristin O'Neill buried a short-handed goal off a two-on-one rush, officially snapping a remarkable 352-minute shutout streak by Frankel. Trailing for the first time all tournament, the Americans aggressively peppered the Canadian net. They outshot Canada 33-31 overall but found themselves running out of time as the third period relentlessly ticked away and Desbiens continued to shut down every high-danger scoring chance. With just 2:04 remaining in regulation and Frankel pulled to give the U.S. a six-on-five skater advantage, the American offense finally cracked the Canadian defense. Cleveland Heights native Laila Edwards, who emerged as a breakout star throughout the tournament, fired a heavy, low shot from the blue line through a maze of traffic. Positioned perfectly in the high slot, 36-year-old captain Hilary Knight expertly redirected the puck through her own legs to tie the game 1-1. The clutch play not only forced overtime but permanently etched Knight into the record books with her 15th career Olympic goal and 33rd Olympic point—both undisputed American records. Knight's equalizer salvaged the gold medal hopes and sent the Milano Santagiulia Ice Hockey Arena into an absolute frenzy. The momentum shifted entirely in the 3-on-3 sudden-death overtime, a format that opened up the ice and heavily favored the American team's exceptional team speed. Just 4:07 into the extra frame, veteran defender Megan Keller took control of the puck off a stretch pass from Taylor Heise and delivered the golden blow. Keller accelerated up the left wing, executed a phenomenal deke to bypass Canadian defender Claire Thompson, and drove hard to the crease. Without hesitation, Keller slid a backhand shot past the goaltender to clinch the championship and spark a massive on-ice celebration. The thrilling finish marks the eighth consecutive U.S. victory over Canada across all competitions and serves as a fitting end to a historic 2026 campaign. While Knight anchored the offense in her fifth and final Olympics, the next generation made their mark. Alongside Edwards' crucial game-tying assist, 23-year-old defender Caroline Harvey earned tournament MVP and Best Defender honors. The victory brings the Olympic gold back to the United States for the third time in program history, capping off a spectacular run that Ohio sports fans and hockey enthusiasts across the nation will not soon forget.

title: Supreme Court Strikes Down Global Tariffs, Citing Limits on Executive Power
description: The Supreme Court handed down a historic 6-3 decision on Friday striking down President Trump's sweeping global tariffs, delivering a profound blow to the administration's economic agenda and fundamentally altering the landscape of international trade.
content: The Supreme Court handed down a historic 6-3 decision on Friday striking down President Trump's sweeping global tariffs, delivering a profound blow to the administration's economic agenda and fundamentally altering the landscape of international trade. Chief Justice John Roberts, authoring the majority opinion alongside the wing of the court, argued that the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 does not grant the executive branch the authority to unilaterally impose taxes or tariffs of unlimited scope and duration. "The Framers did not vest any part of the taxing power in the Executive Branch," Roberts noted. While the adherence to strict constitutional separation of powers is a principle many conservatives deeply respect, the practical reality of this ruling is stark and deeply concerning. It effectively strips the executive branch of its most agile and impactful tool, "tying America's hands behind its back" in high-stakes global trade negotiations. By removing the ability to quickly and decisively use tariffs against adversaries who routinely manipulate markets, the United States has lost a primary bargaining chip essential for protecting domestic industries and American workers. The compelling dissent provided by Justices Brett Kavanaugh, Clarence Thomas, and Samuel Alito correctly highlighted that the text, history, and precedent of the emergency powers law firmly supported the administration's actions. As Justice Kavanaugh noted in his writing, the power to regulate importation naturally encompasses the traditional tool of tariffs, and past presidents have utilized similar emergency powers without facing such judicial obstruction. Without this crucial leverage, foreign competitors are emboldened, knowing they no longer face the immediate threat of reciprocal economic action. The administration's reciprocal tariff strategy was designed to force a "level playing field," and its sudden removal leaves American manufacturing vulnerable to predatory foreign trade practices that have hollowed out the industrial Midwest for decades. Beyond the immediate loss of diplomatic and economic leverage, the fallout from this decision creates immense domestic fiscal chaos. The Treasury Department now faces the monumental and legally complex prospect of refunding billions of dollars in collected duties. Estimates suggest that roughly 130 billion dollars were collected under the IEEPA framework alone over the past year. Processing refunds for thousands of importers will be a logistical nightmare that could take years to resolve. Furthermore, creating such a massive budgetary hole means that taxpayers may ultimately have to bear the burden of this shortfall. The sudden loss of tariff revenue also disrupts the administration's broader fiscal strategy, complicating efforts to balance the budget while maintaining pro-growth tax initiatives. The immediate reaction from the financial sector has been notably short-sighted. While Wall Street and major import-reliant corporations celebrated the ruling with a slight market rally, this temporary relief for multinational conglomerates masks the long-term damage to domestic producers. For areas like the Ohio Valley and other manufacturing hubs, these tariffs were not just abstract economic policy; they were a shield against heavily subsidized foreign steel, auto parts, and consumer goods. Stripping away these protections without a ready replacement leaves local economies exposed to the same globalist policies that previously decimated blue-collar communities. Moving forward, the administration will likely be forced to pivot to alternative, albeit narrower, legal mechanisms to defend American interests. Statutes such as Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act, which focuses on national security, or Section 301, remain intact and will likely become the primary vehicles for future trade enforcement. However, these tools require lengthy bureaucratic investigations and specific findings, lacking the immediate, deterrent impact needed to counter rapidly shifting global threats. The message sent by the Court today severely complicates the broader strategy of negotiating from a "position of strength." If America is to remain a dominant economic force on the world stage, Congress must now step up and explicitly provide the executive branch with the dynamic, undisputed tools necessary to defend our national economic interests in an increasingly hostile global market.

title: Dayton Sues Feds Over PFAS Plume: The $350 Million Battle for Safe Water
description: The City of Dayton escalated its long-standing legal battle with the federal government on Thursday, filing a series of new claims to recover the staggering costs of treating "forever chemicals" that continue to migrate from Wright-Patterson Air Force Base...
content: The City of Dayton escalated its long-standing legal battle with the federal government on Thursday, filing a series of new claims to recover the staggering costs of treating "forever chemicals" that continue to migrate from Wright-Patterson Air Force Base (WPAFB) into the region’s drinking water supply. The lawsuit, filed on February 19, 2026, represents a pivotal moment for the city as it faces a looming federal deadline and a massive infrastructure bill to keep local water safe for over 400,000 residents. The Current Crisis: A Moving Target At the heart of the filing is a "massive plume" of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS). The city alleges that these chemicals—originating from Aqueous Film-Forming Foam (AFFF) used for decades in firefighting training at the base—have leached into the groundwater and are moving directly toward the Mad River Wellfield. While WPAFB has spent approximately $61 million on internal remediation efforts, Dayton city officials argue these actions have been insufficient. The city claims the Air Force has failed to prevent the chemicals from crossing the base boundary, effectively forcing the city to defend its own water source at the taxpayers' expense. The $350 Million Price Tag The financial stakes for the Dayton region are immense. According to city documents and statements from Water Department officials: * $14 Million: Already spent by the city on emergency mitigation and monitoring. * $350 Million: The estimated cost to build a permanent, advanced "end-of-pipe" treatment system. * 2029 Deadline: New U.S. EPA regulations mandate that PFAS levels be reduced to a strict limit of 4 parts per trillion. Without the new facility, the city risks falling out of federal compliance by the end of the decade. City Manager Shelley Dickstein emphasized that the lawsuit is a "necessary step" to protect local rate payers from an "unfair and overwhelming financial burden." A History of Friction This is not Dayton’s first attempt to seek justice. The city originally filed suit against the Department of Defense in 2021 after years of failed negotiations. Despite the base's status as a critical economic engine for the region, the relationship has soured over the DOD's reluctance to fund off-base remediation. In late 2024, the EPA officially updated WPAFB's status, noting that the migration of contaminated groundwater was "not under control." This designation gave the city further leverage, reinforcing their claim that current containment strategies at the base are failing to protect the Great Miami Buried Valley Aquifer. Current Safety and Mitigation Despite the ongoing legal and environmental battle, Dayton’s Department of Water maintains that the finished drinking water remains safe to consume. To achieve this, the city has implemented several stop-gap measures, including shutting down nearly a third of production wells near the Huffman Dam and blending water from unaffected sources to dilute PFAS concentrations. As the case moves through the federal court system, the City of Dayton remains firm: the responsibility for cleaning up legacy military contamination lies with the federal government, not the citizens of the Miami Valley.

title: A Tale of Two Cities: The Lynchpin of Lyon and a Republic on the Brink
description: The visceral, blood-stained streets of Lyon have become the stage for a drama that feels ripped from the pages of Dickens—a stark 'Tale of Two Cities' where political ideology has turned deadly.
content: The visceral, blood-stained streets of Lyon have become the stage for a drama that feels ripped from the pages of Dickens—a stark 'Tale of Two Cities' where political ideology has turned deadly. While the halls of power in Paris attempt to maintain a veneer of Republican order, the internal fabric of France is fraying under the weight of a student’s killing that has ignited a national identity crisis. On February 12, 2026, the Lyon Institute of Political Studies (Sciences Po Lyon) became the site of a brutal confrontation. Quentin Deranque, a 23-year-old mathematics student and activist, was present to support the Nemesis collective—a nationalist feminist group—protesting a conference featuring Rima Hassan, a controversial member of the European Parliament from the hard-left party La France Insoumise (LFI). The situation quickly spiraled. Prosecutors now describe the assault on Deranque as a 'methodically prepared ambush' carried out by masked individuals who vastly outnumbered him. Deranque suffered a fractured skull and severe brain injuries, succumbing to his wounds two days later on February 14. The investigation has sent shockwaves through the French National Assembly. Of the 11 suspects detained by French authorities, seven have been formally charged with intentional homicide and 'complicity by instigation.' Most damagingly, one of the primary suspects is a parliamentary assistant to Raphaël Arnault, an LFI lawmaker and former leader of the militant anti-fascist group Jeune Garde (Young Guard). This direct link to a sitting member of Parliament has pinned the blame on LFI, with critics labeling the party the 'moral sponsors' of far-left violence. The tragedy has crossed borders and ignited a diplomatic spat. Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni called Deranque’s death 'a wound for all of Europe,' sparking a sharp rebuke from President Emmanuel Macron, who told her to 'stay in her own lane' regarding French internal affairs. At home, the climate is radioactive: Marine Le Pen and Jordan Bardella have used the death to demand a total crackdown on 'Antifa' militias, while Jean-Luc Mélenchon has condemned the killing but accused the right of 'demonizing' his party. LFI’s headquarters in Paris was recently evacuated following a bomb threat, underscoring the volatility of the moment. To his supporters, Deranque was a devout Catholic and philosophy enthusiast—a non-violent student defending his convictions. To his detractors on the left, his ties to groups like Action Française and Nemesis marked him as a radicalized militant. Regardless of the label, his death has become the definitive symbol of a country where the 'best of times' for political discourse has rapidly descended into the 'worst of times.'

title: Robots on the Rig: Why the Future of Dayton Construction is Automatic
description: Drywall finishing and painting robots aren't just speculation, they're already here.
content: Drywall finishing and painting robots aren't just speculation, they're already here. If you’ve ever spent a weekend wrestling with a roll of drywall tape or trying to get a smooth finish on a ceiling, you know the truth: drywall finishing is a backbreaking, dusty, and notoriously difficult craft. It’s the kind of work that leaves your shoulders screaming and your lungs full of white powder. But if you walk onto a major job site in the Miami Valley today, you might hear a sound you don't expect—the rhythmic, electronic hum of a robot. The idea of an autonomous machine “mudding,” sanding, and painting walls isn't a scene from a sci-fi movie anymore. It is the new reality of Ohio construction, and it’s changing the way our skyline is built. The technology making waves right now comes from two primary corners of the globe: Petah Tikva, Israel, and San Francisco, California. In Israel, a startup called Okibo has developed the EG7+, a towering, cordless robot that looks like something out of Interstellar. Using 3D laser scanning (LiDAR), it maps a room in seconds, identifies every seam and screw head, and then uses a robotic arm to apply compound with a precision no human hand can match. It can reach 24 feet high without a single piece of scaffolding, doing the work of a three-person crew at triple the speed. Meanwhile, in Silicon Valley, a company called Canvas has taken a “collaborative” approach. Their robots are designed to work alongside local tradespeople, vacuuming up 99.9% of the dust before it ever hits the air. By partnering with industry giants like Hilti and USG (the makers of Sheetrock), they’ve created a system that turns a grueling five-day finishing schedule into a precise 48-hour operation. You might think these “robo-finishers” are stuck in a lab in California or Tel Aviv, but they are already here. Two of the largest construction “titans” in the world are currently deploying this tech on massive projects right in our backyard. Hensel Phelps, the massive general contractor leading the $2 billion New Terminal Project at John Glenn Columbus International Airport, has been an early adopter of Okibo’s autonomous units. Because airport terminals require soaring, perfectly smooth walls, these robots are the only way to keep the project on its aggressive “2026 and beyond” timeline. Closer to home, Performance Contracting Inc. (PCI)—a specialty contractor with a massive presence in the Dayton-Cincinnati corridor—has been utilizing Canvas robots for high-end medical and commercial builds. If you’ve stepped into a newly finished wing of a regional hospital or a tech-heavy office space recently, there is a very high chance a robot performed the final, flawless “Level 5” sand-down. The most mind-blowing part of this story isn't the robots themselves—it’s where they are being used. For decades, we’ve been told that “the future” happens in places like New York or Tokyo. But right now, thanks to the $20 billion Intel “Silicon Heartland” project in New Albany and the massive infrastructure surge across the Miami Valley, Ohio has become the premier global testing ground for construction automation. Because of our unique blend of massive industrial projects and a highly skilled (but aging) workforce, companies from Israel and San Francisco are shipping their newest units to the 937 and 614 area codes before they go anywhere else. We aren't just watching the future of construction from the sidelines; we are the ones building it. The next time you see a new building going up along I-75, take a closer look—the “worker” finishing that top-floor office might just be running on a battery.

title: A Fairy Tale Reimagined: The Sleeping Beauty Takes Center Stage at the Schuster
description: Dayton’s arts scene is set to sparkle this weekend as the Dayton Performing Arts Alliance (DPAA) brings the timeless magic of Tchaikovsky’s The Sleeping Beauty to the Schuster Performing Arts Center.
content: Dayton’s arts scene is set to sparkle this weekend as the Dayton Performing Arts Alliance (DPAA) brings the timeless magic of Tchaikovsky’s The Sleeping Beauty to the Schuster Performing Arts Center. This production is far more than a simple retelling; it marks a significant collaborative milestone for the city's premiere arts organizations, blending professional dance with a full live symphony in a way few cities can replicate. Running through Sunday, February 22, the performance serves as a centerpiece for the 2025–2026 season. While the opening night curtain has already risen, Daytonians still have two opportunities to catch the spectacle: Saturday, February 21 at 7:30 PM and a matinee on Sunday, February 22 at 2:30 PM. Current ticket pricing ranges from $22.50 to $135.00, though availability in the premium orchestra and loge sections is becoming scarce as the weekend progresses. What makes this specific run noteworthy is the new choreography by Brandon Ragland, the Artistic Director of the Dayton Ballet. While Ragland’s vision remains deeply rooted in the classical traditions of Marius Petipa, he has reimagined the staging to create what the DPAA calls an "immersive experience." This version focuses on high-fantasy storytelling and lush scenic design that aim to transport the audience directly into the kingdom of King Florestan. "I’m absolutely thrilled about our '25–26 season," Ragland noted during the season's unveil, emphasizing his goal to bring bold innovation to timeless masterpieces while maintaining the technical rigor the Dayton Ballet is known for. In the pit, the Dayton Philharmonic Orchestra provides the live heartbeat for the production, performing Tchaikovsky’s iconic score in its entirety. This marks a special year for the orchestra as it coincides with Keitaro Harada’s inaugural year as Music and Artistic Director. The seamless integration of the dancers and the live musicians highlights the unique strength of the DPAA, which remains one of the rare unified arts organizations in the country to house a professional ballet, opera, and symphony under a single banner. For those looking to dive deeper into the technical aspects of the dance and score, the DPAA is continuing its tradition of "Take Note" sessions. These live talks occur from the Charles D. Berry Foundation Stage inside the Schuster Center exactly 45 minutes prior to each performance. Led by DPAA artistic leaders and musicians, these 30-minute sessions offer a behind-the-curtain look at the evening's program, exploring the nuances of the choreography and the historical context of Tchaikovsky's composition. It is a perfect primer for both seasoned ballet aficionados and first-time attendees. If you're heading downtown, logistics are straightforward. Parking is recommended at the Arts Garage on the corner of Second and Ludlow Streets. The garage features a covered skywalk that leads directly into the Schuster Center, ensuring a weather-proof entrance for what is expected to be a sold-out weekend of fantasy and folklore. Whether you are coming for the Prologue's Christening or the Grand Pas de Deux of the finale, this production stands as a testament to the thriving cultural heart of Dayton.

title: Wright State Green-Lights $6 Million Expansion to Double Nursing Program Capacity
description: The Wright State University Board of Trustees has signaled a major expansion for the Dayton campus, green-lighting a $6 million investment to modernize its nursing facilities.
content: The Wright State University Board of Trustees has signaled a major expansion for the Dayton campus, green-lighting a $6 million investment to modernize its nursing facilities. The move, finalized during this morning’s board meeting, aims to nearly double the program’s capacity to address the region’s critical nursing shortage. Doubling the Pipeline The investment is designed to scale the Bachelor of Science in Nursing (BSN) program on the Dayton campus. Currently, the program supports 380 students; however, this modernization will allow that number to surge to 740 students by the 2027–2028 academic year. This expansion is a direct response to the "workforce engine" role Wright State plays in Southwest Ohio. By increasing graduate output, the university aims to fill high-demand roles in acute care, primary care, and behavioral health across the Miami Valley. High-Tech Upgrades for University Hall The $6 million will transform the Learning Resource Center in University Hall into a premier training hub. Rather than just adding space, the project focuses on "competency-based education" through several high-tech additions: Virtual Anatomy: A dedicated space for an Anatomage Table, which allows students to perform life-size 3D virtual dissections. Simulation Labs: Two new medical simulation rooms equipped with the latest software to mimic real-world clinical scenarios. Advanced Equipment: The fleet of training mannequins will be upgraded to include low-, mid-, and high-fidelity models, capable of simulating a wide range of medical emergencies. Clinical Space: The renovation includes the addition of 42 new nursing beds, mirroring the environment students will encounter in local hospitals. Strategic Partnerships & Timeline The project reinforces Wright State’s strategic partnership with Premier Health, ensuring that local healthcare providers have a steady influx of highly trained professionals. To minimize the impact on current students, the university has scheduled the construction in two major phases: Phase 1: Set to begin in Summer 2026. Phase 2: Scheduled for Summer 2027. Full Launch: The modernized facilities are expected to be fully operational for the start of the Fall 2027 semester. Interim Board Chair John Thompson emphasized that this investment is about more than just classrooms—it’s about stabilizing the regional healthcare infrastructure. As Dayton continues to face a vacancy crisis in nursing, Wright State is positioning itself as the primary solution for the city's medical future.

title: RingCentral Shares Skyrocket 35% on Record Profitability and First-Ever Dividend
description: RingCentral ( RNG ) shares experienced a massive breakout during Friday's trading session, surging more than 35% to hit a new 52-week high of approximately $39.81.
content: RingCentral (RNG) shares experienced a massive breakout during Friday's trading session, surging more than 35% to hit a new 52-week high of approximately $39.81. The aggressive rally follows a blockbuster Q4 2025 earnings report that signaled a fundamental shift for the cloud communications giant, transitioning from a high-growth tech play into a highly profitable, cash-generative powerhouse. For the fourth quarter, RingCentral reported adjusted earnings of $1.18 per share, soundly beating the analyst consensus of $0.91. Revenue climbed to $644 million, underpinned by a 6% increase in subscription revenue. However, the true catalyst for the stock's vertical move was the company's aggressive 2026 guidance and a surprise update to its capital allocation strategy. Management now expects 2026 adjusted earnings to land between $4.76 and $4.97 per share, far exceeding the $3.85 Wall Street had projected. In a historic move, the Board of Directors initiated the company’s first-ever quarterly cash dividend of $0.075 per share, payable on March 16. This milestone, paired with a boosted share repurchase authorization of $500 million, reflects a level of financial maturity that investors have long awaited. CEO Vlad Shmunis also highlighted the accelerating impact of AI on the bottom line, noting that Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) from AI-monetized products nearly tripled year-over-year and now accounts for roughly 10% of total ARR. Beyond the fundamentals, technical demand played a secondary role as the stock recently joined the S&P SmallCap 600 index, forcing passive funds to build positions. With free cash flow reaching a record $530 million in 2025 and a clear roadmap to double GAAP operating margins in 2026, RingCentral has effectively quieted skeptics who questioned its ability to scale profitably in an AI-driven landscape. This 'perfect storm' of a dividend launch, a massive earnings beat, and robust AI adoption has positioned RNG as one of the top tech performers of the quarter.

title: Markets End The Week Higher: Friday, Feb 20
description: U.S. equity markets finished Friday in positive territory, securing a gain to close out the trading week as investors reacted to a landmark Supreme Court ruling and fresh economic data.
content: U.S. equity markets finished Friday in positive territory, securing a gain to close out the trading week as investors reacted to a landmark Supreme Court ruling and fresh economic data. The S&P 500 climbed 0.7% to finish at 6,861.89, while the Nasdaq Composite led the gains with a 0.9% rise to 22,682.73. The Dow Jones Industrial Average also moved higher, adding 0.5% to close at 49,395.16. This upward momentum followed a period of uncertainty, as market participants weighed the implications of a cooling domestic economy against a significant shift in federal trade policy. The primary driver for Friday's session was the U.S. Supreme Court’s 6-3 decision to strike down broad 'reciprocal' tariffs previously enacted under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The ruling effectively removes a major layer of fiscal uncertainty for the retail and technology sectors, which had been bracing for multi-billion dollar cost increases. Shares of major importers like Amazon, Meta, and Google saw a relief rally following the news. While the administration has signaled a potential shift toward more targeted, country-specific trade measures, the immediate removal of these broad levies provided a clear catalyst for the day's gains. Simultaneously, the Bureau of Economic Analysis released its Advance Estimate for Q4 2025 GDP, revealing a significant deceleration in growth. The U.S. economy expanded at an annualized rate of 1.4% in the final quarter of the year, a sharp drop from the 4.4% growth seen in Q3. Despite this cooling, the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) price index held steady at 2.6%. This combination of slower growth and persistent inflation suggests the Federal Reserve remains in a delicate position, as the data does not yet provide a clear mandate for immediate interest rate cuts. Beyond the headline indexes, Silver (XAG/USD) surged over 7.5% as industrial demand expectations shifted in light of the trade ruling. In the corporate sector, Comfort Systems rose 2.4% following a strong earnings report. As the market looks toward next week, the focus is shifting to the potential for 'IEEPA refunds'—the billions in tariffs already collected that may now be owed back to corporations. If these refunds materialize, they could act as a substantial liquidity injection for the private sector, potentially serving as a catalyst for future investment.

title: Damascus Rising: The Phased Reintegration of a Post-War Syria
description: DAMASCUS — In a move that signals the most significant diplomatic shift in the Middle East this decade, the Trump administration officially notified Congress this week of its intent to reopen the U.S.
content: DAMASCUS — In a move that signals the most significant diplomatic shift in the Middle East this decade, the Trump administration officially notified Congress this week of its intent to reopen the U.S. Embassy in Damascus. Shuttered for fourteen years, the potential return of a full U.S. diplomatic mission marks a "new chapter" for a nation once defined by a brutal, decade-long civil war, now under the transitional leadership of President Ahmed al-Sharaa. The Diplomatic Pivot The rapprochement between Washington and Damascus has accelerated rapidly following the 2024 ouster of the Assad regime. U.S. Special Envoy Tom Barrack has been the primary architect of this "deep rapprochement," successfully advocating for the lifting of major sanctions and the restoration of Syria to the international community. Just today, President Trump lauded the stability under al-Sharaa, describing the Syrian leader as "phenomenal" and crediting him with bringing a fractured country back together. This diplomatic thaw coincides with the planned phased reopening of the embassy; funding for the project is slated to begin next week, though no definitive timeline for the return of full-time personnel has been released. A New National Army The cornerstone of Syria's internal stability is the January 30, 2026, agreement between the central government and the Kurdish-led Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF). This landmark deal has effectively ended the "state-within-a-state" model in the northeast. SDF units are currently being folded into three dedicated brigades under the Syrian Ministry of Defense. Additionally, autonomous administrative institutions are being absorbed into state frameworks, with Kurdish residents retaining local administrative roles to preserve regional identity. Control of the country’s vital oil and gas fields in Deir ez-Zor and Raqqa has been formally transferred back to the central government. This integration follows a full U.S. military withdrawal. As of February 20, the U.S. has completed the handover of the strategic al-Tanf base and the al-Shaddadi garrison. Roughly 2,000 U.S. troops are expected to be entirely out of the country within two months, leaving the burden of counterterrorism to al-Sharaa’s new national force. The Closing of al-Hol The humanitarian landscape is also undergoing a radical transformation. The infamous al-Hol camp, which for years served as a holding pen for tens of thousands of ISIS-linked families, is in the final stages of closure. Most remaining residents have been moved to a new facility in Akhtarin, northern Aleppo, which offers better infrastructure and centralized services. To mitigate the risk of a mass breakout during the transition, the U.S. military concluded a massive operation today to transfer approximately 5,700 high-risk ISIS fighters from Syrian prisons to Iraq for trial. Reconciliation and Reconstruction President al-Sharaa has moved to consolidate his domestic legitimacy through a series of sweeping decrees. Decree No. 39, issued on February 18, granted a general amnesty for crimes committed prior to that date, aimed at encouraging the return of millions of displaced citizens. Further efforts to mend ethnic divisions include the annulment of the 1962 census measures that left thousands of Kurds stateless. Kurdish has been recognized as a national language, and al-Sharaa has publicly framed the Kurdish population as a "basic part" of the Syrian people. The Challenges Ahead Despite these gains, the path forward is marred by economic and environmental fragility. This February, severe rainfall caused catastrophic flooding in northern Syria, displacing hundreds of families already living in temporary shelters. While authorities have launched a massive campaign in Idlib to remove over 600,000 cubic meters of war debris, the infrastructure remains crippled. With over 1.4 million refugees having returned since late 2024, the pressure on al-Sharaa to provide basic services remains the greatest test of his transitional government.

title: DOT Orders Immediate Closure of 557 Commercial Driving Schools
description: The U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) has announced an aggressive enforcement action, ordering the immediate closure or removal of 557 commercial driving schools from the National Training Provider Registry (TPR).
content: The U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT) has announced an aggressive enforcement action, ordering the immediate closure or removal of 557 commercial driving schools from the National Training Provider Registry (TPR). This move, announced by Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy on Wednesday, February 18, 2026, marks the first major enforcement of the 2022 safety standards and aims to purge 'CDL mills' that have prioritized profit over public safety. The crackdown is the result of 1,426 on-site visits conducted by the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) in December 2025. Of the total schools affected, 448 providers were issued notices of proposed removal after failing to meet basic safety standards, while another 109 schools chose to voluntarily withdraw from the registry upon learning that federal investigators were en route to their facilities. An additional 97 schools currently remain under investigation for potential compliance issues. Investigators identified a wide range of critical safety failures during the audits. Among the most frequent violations were the use of unqualified instructors who lacked the necessary licenses for the vehicles they were teaching and the use of substandard equipment, such as training students for heavy-duty Class A licenses in vehicles that did not meet weight requirements. Furthermore, many schools were found to be skipping mandatory hazardous materials (HazMat) training and failing to conduct rigorous skills testing, often relying on a 'self-certification' loophole that regulators are now closing. This national enforcement effort follows a series of tragic, high-profile crashes, including a fatal accident in Florida and a recent crash in Indiana that killed four people. Secretary Duffy emphasized that the administration is also tightening English proficiency standards for all commercial drivers to ensure they can understand road signs and safety regulations. States that fail to cooperate with these new enforcement measures face significant financial consequences; the DOT has already threatened to withhold millions in federal highway funds, with California already seeing a $160 million reduction. While the removal of over 500 schools is significant, industry analysts suggest that a 10% drop in national shipment volumes since 2022 provides a temporary cushion for the driver pipeline. However, the American Trucking Associations (ATA) and the Owner-Operator Independent Drivers Association (OOIDA) have both praised the move, stating that rooting out bad actors is essential for maintaining the integrity and professionalism of the trucking industry moving forward.

title: Sweeps, Citations, and Safety: Dayton PD Announces Curfews
description: The Dayton Police Department (DPD) announced a significant enforcement initiative today, Friday, Feb. 20, aimed at curbing late-night juvenile activity across the city.
content: The Dayton Police Department (DPD) announced a significant enforcement initiative today, Friday, Feb. 20, aimed at curbing late-night juvenile activity across the city. Beginning immediately and scheduled to continue through the remainder of February and March, officers will conduct targeted "curfew sweeps" designed to remove unsupervised minors from public spaces during overnight hours. The announcement signals a shift toward stricter adherence to existing city ordinances, under which anyone under the age of 18 is prohibited from loitering, idling, or "loafing" in public areas—including streets, alleys, and city parks—between the hours of 11 p.m. and 5 a.m., unless accompanied by a parent or legal guardian. The DPD has outlined specific protocols for these upcoming operations. Juveniles detained during a sweep will not simply be instructed to return home. Instead, officers will transport them directly to the East Patrol District headquarters located at 2721 Wayne Ave. The onus will then fall on parents or legal guardians to travel to the station to retrieve their minors. Police officials emphasized that if a guardian cannot be successfully contacted or located within a reasonable timeframe, the juvenile will be transported to the Juvenile Detention Center for holding until someone responsible can be reached. Department officials framed the initiative today not merely as an enforcement blitz, but as a necessary proactive public safety strategy bolstered by concerning local data. The DPD cited statistics indicating that in each of the last three years—2023, 2024, and 2025—more than 80 juveniles in Dayton were victims of violent crimes, ranging from aggravated robbery and assault to murder. The stated goal of the sweeps is to reduce these victimization rates by ensuring youths are off the streets during high-risk hours. Understanding the Consequences: More Than a Warning Perhaps the most significant aspect of today's announcement for local families is the confirmation that these sweeps will result in immediate legal ramifications. This is not a warning program. If a teen is caught violating curfew during a sweep, police will issue citations to both the juvenile and their parent or guardian. The DPD also stated that alongside the ticket, they intend to provide parents with a list of community resources aimed at supporting families and keeping youths engaged in positive activities. The citations will be issued as minor misdemeanors. Under Ohio law, a minor misdemeanor is the lowest level of criminal offense. It is distinct because it carries no possibility of jail time for the violation itself; it is considered a "fine-only" offense. The maximum base fine for a minor misdemeanor is $150. However, those cited should anticipate additional financial burdens, as court costs and administrative fees are mandatory and frequently exceed the amount of the actual fine. Judges in Dayton municipal court also retain discretion in how these cases are resolved. Under state law, a judge may order up to 30 hours of community service in lieu of, or in addition to, the financial penalties. The Long-Term Impact For concerned parents and older teens looking toward college or employment, the impact on permanent records is often a primary anxiety. Generally, under Ohio law, a minor misdemeanor conviction does not constitute a formal criminal conviction record in the same manner as a theft or assault charge. It is often treated similarly to a minor traffic violation in background screenings. However, the citation does become a public record. Those wishing to clear their history may apply to have the record sealed or expunged, a process that typically becomes available six months to one year after the case is closed and all fines are paid. While the curfew citation itself carries no threat of incarceration, legal experts warn of a critical pitfall for parents who receive a summons. Ignoring the ticket changes the legal landscape drastically. Failing to pay the prescribed fine or failing to appear in court on the designated date can result in a separate, more serious charge of "Failure to Appear." Unlike the curfew violation, a Failure to Appear charge can lead to an arrest warrant and potential jail time for the adult responsible.

title: The Showdown in Milan: USA and Canada Set for Historic Gold Medal Clash
description: MILAN, Italy — It’s the matchup everyone was hoping for. For the first time in 16 years, the United States and Canada will face off in the Olympic men’s hockey gold medal game.
content: MILAN, Italy — It’s the matchup everyone was hoping for. For the first time in 16 years, the United States and Canada will face off in the Olympic men’s hockey gold medal game. The showdown is set for Sunday, February 22, 2026, at the Milano Santagiulia Ice Hockey Arena. Both teams have taken vastly different paths to reach this final. While the Americans have relied on a high-octane, dominant offense, the Canadians have proven to be the "Cardiac Kids" of the tournament, surviving several late-game scares. Team USA: The "No Miracle Needed" Run The Americans have been the most dominant team in Milan, outscoring their opponents 24–8 throughout the tournament. While they’ve played with flair, they’ve also shown they can grind out tough wins. The turning point for the U.S. came in the quarterfinals against Sweden. After a back-and-forth battle, the U.S. needed overtime to survive. Quinn Hughes sealed the win with a "ripper" of a shot in the 3-on-3 session to send the Americans to the semis. They followed that up with a statement 6-2 win over Slovakia, where Jack Hughes scored two highlight-reel goals and Zach Werenski facilitated the offense with three assists. Between the pipes, goaltender Connor Hellebuyck has been a wall, validating the decision to start him over other NHL stars like Jake Oettinger and Jeremy Swayman. Team Canada: The "Cardiac Kids" Comebacks Canada’s path has been much more stressful. They’ve developed a reputation for late-game heroics and power-play efficiency. In the quarterfinals against Czechia, Canada trailed twice and nearly faced a massive upset before pulling out a 4-3 win in overtime. However, the victory came at a cost: Captain Sidney Crosby suffered a lower-body injury and has been sidelined since. In the semifinals against Finland, Canada fell behind 2-0 early. They didn't panic. Sam Reinhart and Shea Theodore tied it up, and Nathan MacKinnon scored the game-winner on a power play with just 35.2 seconds left in regulation. Connor McDavid, who took over the captaincy in Crosby’s absence, currently holds the record for the most points in a single Olympic tournament with NHL players (13 points). The Stakes & The Rivalry This isn't just about 2026; it's about a long-standing history of North American dominance. The U.S. hasn't won Olympic gold since the "Miracle on Ice" in 1980, while Canada is looking to reclaim the top spot they last held in 2014. The big question heading into Sunday remains the health of Sidney Crosby. Canadian coach Jon Cooper mentioned he has a better chance of playing the final than he did the semi, but his presence—or absence—will be the defining storyline of the morning.

title: North Dayton Garage Fire Sends Smoke Over U.S. 35
description: Dayton fire crews battled a significant garage fire Friday evening in the 400 block of Eleanor Avenue , an incident so visible it caught the attention of commuters miles away.
content: Dayton fire crews battled a significant garage fire Friday evening in the 400 block of Eleanor Avenue, an incident so visible it caught the attention of commuters miles away. The blaze was first reported shortly before 7:00 p.m. in the North Dayton neighborhood. As firefighters arrived on the scene, they were met with heavy flames venting from a garage structure. The intensity of the fire produced a thick, black plume of smoke that was clearly visible to drivers traveling along U.S. 35 and throughout much of the surrounding Miami Valley. Firefighters utilized multiple hose lines to establish a perimeter, successfully preventing the intense heat and flames from spreading to the primary residence on the property or neighboring homes. A spokesperson for the Dayton Fire Department confirmed that the fire was extinguished within a few hours. Fortunately, no injuries have been reported in connection with the incident. The cause of the fire remains under investigation as fire investigators continue to comb through the debris. Residents in the area described the scene as "startling," noting the rapid response from local emergency services that kept the situation from escalating.

title: Lithium-Ion Batteries Suspected in Massive Texas Recycling Fire
description: DAYTON, Texas — A massive industrial fire that sent plumes of toxic black smoke over Southeast Texas this week has been successfully extinguished, leaving investigators to point toward a familiar modern culprit: lithium-ion batteries .
content: DAYTON, Texas — A massive industrial fire that sent plumes of toxic black smoke over Southeast Texas this week has been successfully extinguished, leaving investigators to point toward a familiar modern culprit: lithium-ion batteries. The blaze broke out at approximately 7:00 p.m. on Tuesday, February 17, at the OmniSource recycling facility. Located within the burgeoning Gulf Inland Logistics Park off Riverbend Way, the site is a critical hub for processing "end-of-life" consumer goods. What began as a localized hotspot quickly evolved into a multi-day emergency that drew national attention and tested the resources of nearly 20 regional fire departments. The Anatomy of the Blaze The fire’s primary fuel source was a 60-foot-tall mountain of shredded household appliances—a dense mixture of washing machines, dryers, and refrigerators. Because the material was tightly packed, the fire became "deep-seated," burning within the core of the pile where traditional water streams could not easily reach. Emergency crews eventually pivoted to a "defensive posture," drenching surrounding piles of scrap and vehicles to prevent the fire from leaping to more hazardous materials. The intensity of the smoke prompted the Liberty County Office of Emergency Management to issue a mandatory shelter-in-place for all residents within a five-mile radius north of U.S. 90. The Battery Culprit While a final report from the Liberty County Fire Marshal is pending, officials have identified lithium-ion batteries—improperly discarded inside recycled appliances—as the most likely ignition source. "The biggest challenge in modern recycling isn't the metal; it's the hidden power sources," noted one fire official. "A single damaged battery under the weight of a 60-foot pile can generate enough heat to ignite the surrounding plastics and insulation." The incident highlights a growing national trend of "thermal runaway" events at recycling centers, where batteries from laptops, tools, and small electronics are crushed during processing, sparking fires that are notoriously difficult to suppress. Current Status and Recovery As of Friday, February 20, the site is stable. The shelter-in-place was officially lifted at 5:00 a.m. on Wednesday after mobile air monitoring units confirmed that particulate levels had returned to safe thresholds. Remarkably, despite the scale of the inferno, no injuries were reported among facility staff, local residents, or the hundreds of first responders on the scene. The OmniSource facility, a subsidiary of Steel Dynamics, only recently established its footprint in the 55-acre Gulf Inland Logistics Park. This incident is expected to prompt renewed discussions regarding safety protocols for "pre-shredded" scrap storage and the increasing difficulty of screening for hazardous battery waste in the recycling stream.

title: College Basketball’s Last Perfect Team Marches On as Miami (OH) Hits 27-0
description: OXFORD, Ohio — The pressure of perfection is known to crack even the most seasoned college basketball blue bloods. Yet, tucked away in the Mid-American Conference, the Miami University RedHawks continue to make history look entirely routine.
content: OXFORD, Ohio — The pressure of perfection is known to crack even the most seasoned college basketball blue bloods. Yet, tucked away in the Mid-American Conference, the Miami University RedHawks continue to make history look entirely routine. On Friday night, college basketball’s last remaining undefeated team systematically dismantled Bowling Green 91-77 at Millett Hall. With the victory, No. 22 Miami (OH) improved to a staggering 27-0 on the season, elevating their campaign from a great mid-major storyline to a legitimate national phenomenon. In a sport traditionally dominated by major conference powerhouses, the RedHawks have become the undisputed Cinderella story of the 2025-26 season. The national implications are impossible to ignore: Not since the 2020-21 Gonzaga Bulldogs has a Division I men’s basketball program opened a season with 27 consecutive victories. Bowling Green entered the matchup hoping to play spoiler, leaning heavily on the playmaking of guard Javontae Campbell to disrupt the RedHawks' rhythm. However, head coach Travis Steele’s high-octane offense—currently leading the nation in scoring—proved far too multifaceted to contain. "Our group of boys just kind of stays flatline," Steele noted earlier this week. "We're unflappable." The RedHawks absorbed the Falcons' best punches and responded with blistering execution. Veterans like Peter Suder and Brant Byers dictated the pace, consistently getting to the rim and converting at the free-throw line, while Eian Elmer provided crucial perimeter shooting to stretch the floor. Whenever Bowling Green attempted a second-half rally, Miami answered with the composure of a Final Four contender, methodically widening the gap to a comfortable 14-point margin by the final buzzer. What makes Miami’s run so compelling on the national stage is how thoroughly they are dominating their competition. While skeptics will naturally point to their MAC schedule, the RedHawks are passing the eye test and rapidly climbing the national analytics rankings. Their offensive efficiency is elite, and their ability to manufacture points in late-clock situations makes them a terrifying matchup for any prospective opponent in the upcoming NCAA Tournament. With the regular season winding down, the conversation around the RedHawks has firmly shifted to serious bracketology. This program hasn't been ranked this late in the year since 1999, back when Wally Szczerbiak led them on a magical run to the Sweet 16. The 2026 iteration looks fully capable of replicating—or even exceeding—that kind of deep March run. Every team in the country now has a target on Miami. But if Friday night’s statement victory is any indication, the RedHawks aren't feeling the pressure. They are simply enjoying the ride.

title: Miami Valley Convention Bureau Works to Attract Visitors and Boost Local Economy
description: The Miami County Visitors and Convention Bureau (MCVCB) continues its mission of promoting the Miami Valley region as a destination for tourism, events, and business travel. The bureau's ongoing efforts aim to drive economic activity across Montgomery, Miami, and surrounding counties. Local hospitality businesses and community stakeholders stand to benefit as the organization refines its regional storytelling strategy.
content:
The Miami County Visitors and Convention Bureau (MCVCB) is doubling down on its efforts to position the Miami Valley as a compelling destination for tourists, event planners, and business travelers — a mission that carries real economic weight for the Dayton region and its surrounding communities.

The bureau, which serves as the official destination marketing organization for the area, has been actively working to craft and amplify a cohesive narrative about what makes the Miami Valley worth visiting, worth investing in, and worth returning to. That work spans digital marketing campaigns, outreach to convention planners, partnerships with local attractions, and coordination with hospitality businesses ranging from independent bed-and-breakfasts to major hotel chains.

Destination marketing organizations like the MCVCB operate largely behind the scenes, but their impact on local economies can be substantial. When the bureau successfully books a regional conference, a sporting tournament, or a group tour package, the ripple effects are felt across hotels, restaurants, retail shops, and entertainment venues throughout the area. A single mid-sized convention can generate hundreds of thousands of dollars in direct visitor spending over just a few days.

The Miami Valley region has a rich story to tell. From the Wright Brothers' legacy in Dayton — one of the most significant chapters in American technological history — to the natural beauty of the Great Miami River corridor, the area offers a mix of cultural heritage, outdoor recreation, and modern amenities that many visitors overlook in favor of larger metropolitan markets. The MCVCB's challenge, and its opportunity, lies in changing that perception.

Dayton's revitalized downtown has added new dimensions to that story in recent years. The growth of the Oregon District, the expansion of the Dayton Dragons' presence at Day Air Ballpark, and continued investment in the city's arts and culinary scenes have given the bureau more material to work with when pitching the region to outside audiences. The National Museum of the United States Air Force at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base remains one of the most-visited free museums in the country and serves as a cornerstone of the region's tourism identity.

Beyond Dayton proper, the bureau also promotes assets across Miami County, including the city of Troy and its well-regarded downtown, the Piqua historical sites, and the broader rural landscape that draws cyclists, hikers, and agritourism enthusiasts. The Miami Valley's position along several major interstate corridors makes it accessible to large population centers including Columbus, Cincinnati, Indianapolis, and Columbus — a geographic advantage the MCVCB actively markets to drive day-trip and weekend-getaway traffic.

The hospitality industry in the Miami Valley, like much of the country, is still navigating the long-term shifts in travel behavior that emerged from the pandemic era. Business travel has not fully rebounded to pre-2020 levels nationally, and competition among regional destinations for leisure travelers has intensified. In that environment, the MCVCB's ability to differentiate the Miami Valley — and to tell its story effectively across digital platforms, travel trade publications, and direct sales channels — becomes more critical than ever.

Local hotel occupancy rates and tourism tax revenues serve as key benchmarks for measuring the bureau's effectiveness. Those figures feed directly into municipal and county budgets, since lodging taxes typically fund a portion of the MCVCB's operating budget while also supporting parks, infrastructure, and community programming. When visitor numbers rise, so does the public revenue available to local governments.

The bureau's work also intersects with broader economic development goals for the region. Attracting a major conference or relocating a corporate event to Dayton can introduce business decision-makers to the area in ways that occasionally lead to longer-term investment conversations. In a region that has worked hard to diversify its economic base beyond its traditional manufacturing roots, every positive impression made on an outside visitor carries potential value beyond the immediate spending.

Community stakeholders — including local chambers of commerce, arts organizations, sports venues, and hospitality businesses — generally view the MCVCB as a valuable partner, though destination marketing organizations in many cities face periodic scrutiny over how public lodging tax dollars are allocated and whether marketing strategies are producing measurable returns.

As the MCVCB continues refining its approach to telling Miami Valley's story, the organization's success will ultimately be measured not just in marketing metrics, but in the tangible economic activity it helps generate for the businesses and residents of the Dayton region.

title: British Government Weighs Removing Prince Andrew From Succession After Arrest
description: The British government is reportedly considering legislation to formally remove Prince Andrew from the line of succession to the crown following his arrest in connection with an ongoing investigation. The move would mark a significant constitutional step and deepen the royal family's break from the disgraced former duke. King Charles III has already distanced himself from his younger brother, who lost his royal titles and military patronages in 2022.
content:
The British government is actively considering removing Prince Andrew, the Duke of York, from the official line of succession to the crown following his arrest, according to multiple reports from CBS News, Sky News, and other major outlets. The development represents one of the most dramatic constitutional actions taken against a senior royal figure in modern British history and signals that the fallout from Andrew's long-running association with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein has entered a new and more consequential phase.

Andrew, who was formally stripped of the title "Prince" and his royal duties in early 2022 amid civil sexual assault allegations brought by Virginia Giuffre — a case that was later settled out of court with no admission of wrongdoing — now faces renewed scrutiny. Searches were reportedly conducted at his former residence, Royal Lodge in Windsor Great Park, as part of an ongoing investigation.
