-July 4th: The annual Independence Day celebration remains the fastest-selling date on the calendar. -July 18: The 16th Annual Dragons 5K (registration also opened this morning). -Sunday Games: "Kids Run the Bases" returns for every Sunday home game, offering post-game field access to children 11 and under.
imageCaption: Aerial view of Day-Air Ballpark in downtown Dayton, Ohio - home of the Dayton Dragons.

title: Kettering Police Seek Leads in Valentine’s Day Locker Room Theft at Planet Fitness
description: The Kettering Police Department is seeking public assistance to identify two women involved in a Valentine’s Day felony theft at the Wilmington Pike Planet Fitness. The suspects, seen in a black Chevy Silverado, allegedly stole IDs and medication from the locker room before using stolen credit cards at Discount Fashion Warehouse. Contact Ptl. Douglas at (937) 296-2555 with any leads.
content:
KETTERING, Ohio — The Kettering Police Department is asking for public assistance in identifying two individuals wanted in connection with a felony theft that occurred on Valentine’s Day.

According to a surveillance advisory issued by the department, the incident took place on February 14 at the Planet Fitness located on Wilmington Pike. Police state that two female suspects arrived at the location in a black Chevy Silverado.

After entering the facility, the suspects reportedly accessed the locker room and stole personal identification, medication, and credit cards. Kettering police further allege that the suspects subsequently used one of the stolen credit cards at a Discount Fashion Warehouse location.

The department has released two surveillance images of the suspects taken on the day of the incident. In one image, a female is seen wearing a lavender or light purple hoodie and dark pants. The other image shows a female wearing a dark-colored hoodie or crewneck sweatshirt, dark pants, and distinctive red shoes.

Investigators are asking anyone with information regarding the identity of these individuals or the incident to contact the Kettering Police Department. Leads can be directed to Patrolman Douglas at (937) 296-2555. When calling, please reference police report number 26-006125.
imageCaption: Two female suspects seen leaving the Planet Fitness in Kettering, Ohio - Feb. 14 2026

title: Dayton Monday Guide: $1 Oysters, $3 Burgers, and a Scotch Doubles Shootout
description: Dayton is buzzing this Monday, March 2, with deals across the Gem City. Enjoy $1 oysters all day at Club Oceano in Beavercreek or grab a $3 burger at Bullwinkle’s Top Hat Bistro starting at 5:00 PM. Competitive spirits can join the Scotch Doubles pool tournament at Miami Valley Sports Bar. Plus, Dayton Dragons 2026 single-game tickets are officially on sale.
content:
Monday blues? Not in the Gem City. While Mondays are usually reserved for settling back into the work week, local businesses across Dayton are making it easy (and affordable) to break the routine today, Monday, March 2nd.

Whether you’re in the mood for an all-day seafood feast, a classic burger in historic downtown Miamisburg, or a competitive round of pool, here is your essential guide to navigating the best local events and culinary deals happening around town.

On the Menu: High-End Oysters to Classic Burgers

If you're looking to upgrade your lunch hour or save money on dinner, two local staples are running major specials today.

$1 Oysters at Club Oceano (Beavercreek) Seafood lovers will want to head toward The Greene. Club Oceano is running their popular $1 oyster special all day from 11:00 AM to 9:00 PM. To get the special pricing, you must order your oysters in groups of six. While the deal runs all day, the raw bar tends to get busiest during their standard happy hour from 3:00 PM to 6:00 PM, so plan accordingly if you want to beat the rush.

$3 (and up) Burgers at Bullwinkle’s Top Hat Bistro (Miamisburg) Later this afternoon, the focus shifts to a legendary downtown Miamisburg establishment. Bullwinkle’s, known for its iconic "Q" sauce and relaxed, classic atmosphere, kicks off its popular burger night at 5:00 PM, running until 10:00 PM.

This tiered special allows you to build your own deal. A classic plain burger on a bun starts at $3, while $4 adds the works including cheese, lettuce, tomato, and onion. For $5, you can upgrade to one of their specialty burger combinations. As a reminder, street parking directly on Main Street near Bullwinkle’s often fills up fast on burger night. Drivers may want to utilize the public parking lots located just one block over.

For the Competitors: Scotch Doubles Tournament

If you are looking for action over dinner, or just want to test your skill, the Miami Valley Sports Bar is hosting a high-energy tournament tonight.

The Details: "Justin’s Famous LOTD"

This isn’t your standard singles pool matchup. Tonight is "Scotch Doubles" (Luck of the Draw). In the Scotch Doubles format, teams of two alternate shots rather than alternate turns, requiring strategy and adaptability between partners.

The official draw begins at 7:15 PM, with the tournament starting at 7:30 PM. There is a $10 buy-in, but importantly, the bar matches the pot 100 percent. VNEA rules will apply for all matches.

Also Noted in Dayton Today

Baseball season is fast approaching. Dayton Dragons single-game tickets for the 2026 season officially went on sale this morning at 10:00 AM. Since the Dragons hold the continuous record for professional sports sellouts, fans hoping to catch prime weekend games starting with Opening Night on April 7th are encouraged to secure seats today.

Additionally, if you are visiting Beavercreek for oysters but prefer a mental workout, The Pub at The Greene is hosting its popular weekly Quiz Night starting at 6:30 PM.

Stay tuned to your Dayton news source for more local updates and events happening throughout the week.
imageCaption: Club Oceano seafood and bar in Beavercreek, Ohio offers $1 oysters every Monday.

title: CDC Data Shows Prenatal Care Is Starting Later Across the U.S., Raising New Maternal-Health Alarms
description: New federal data shows first-trimester prenatal care fell to 75.5% in 2024 while late or no care rose, signaling a broad access and timing problem with real downstream risk.
content:
A new federal data brief shows a clear and troubling shift in U.S. maternal care timing: first-trimester prenatal care fell to 75.5% in 2024, down from 78.3% in 2021, while second-trimester starts and late-or-no-care rates both climbed. In practical terms, prenatal care is starting later for more families, and that trend is moving in the wrong direction after years of relative stability.

The headline percentages are straightforward but consequential. From 2021 to 2024, second-trimester initiation rose from 15.4% to 17.3%, and late or no care rose from 6.3% to 7.3%. Late-or-no-care rates increased in 36 states and the District of Columbia, and more than one in ten mothers fell into that category in several jurisdictions. These are not isolated local anomalies; they point to a national timing problem with broad geographic footprint.

Why timing matters is well understood in clinical practice. Early prenatal contact is when providers confirm baseline risk, identify hypertension and diabetes concerns, review medications, screen for infection, and establish continuity plans before complications escalate. When care starts later, intervention windows narrow. That can translate into more emergency management and fewer preventive opportunities, which is expensive for systems and risky for families.

The demographic pattern is also instructive. Declines in first-trimester initiation were seen across all maternal age groups from 2021 to 2024. The steepest declines appeared among younger mothers, who already face structural barriers around transportation, scheduling flexibility, insurance transitions, and childcare logistics. Racial and ethnic disparities persisted as well, with some groups experiencing sharper deterioration in early access than others.

This is where maternal-health policy often misses the target. Public debate tends to focus on hospital-level outcomes at delivery, but access pressure often begins much earlier in the pipeline: appointment availability, coverage friction, administrative burden, and local provider shortages. If entry into care keeps drifting later, improvements in labor-and-delivery protocols alone cannot fully offset the downstream risk.

For health systems, the message is operational. The metric that deserves more attention is not just whether prenatal care occurs, but when it starts. Network planning, reimbursement incentives, and outreach models are more likely to improve outcomes if they are tied to first-contact timing, not only total encounter counts. In other words, “care delivered” is not enough if care begins after the highest-leverage preventive window has already passed.

For policymakers, the trend is a warning against complacency in a period where maternal-health rhetoric is high but access infrastructure remains uneven. If late-or-no-care rates continue rising, systems may see more avoidable acuity, wider disparities, and higher long-run cost per pregnancy episode. Reversing that trajectory will require less symbolic messaging and more practical friction removal at the front door of care.

The immediate story is a data movement from one set of percentages to another. The deeper story is that prenatal care timing is functioning as an early stress indicator for U.S. maternal-health capacity, and right now that indicator is flashing caution.
imageCaption: The map illustrates the percentage of mothers who received late or no prenatal care by state in 2024, alongside the change in these rates from 2021 to 2024.

title: Tragedy on I-75: Father and Son Killed in Multi-Vehicle Crash
description: A tragic six-vehicle crash on I-75 near Dayton has claimed the lives of a father and son. The multi-vehicle pileup, occurring late Saturday night in Allen County, involved two semis and four passenger cars. Amanda Gipson remains hospitalized with serious injuries. This heartbreaking event headlines a somber weekend for the Miami Valley following several other local tragedies and investigations.
content:
The Dayton area is mourning the loss of a father and son who were killed in a horrific six-vehicle pileup on Interstate 75 late Saturday night. The crash, which involved commercial semis and passenger vehicles, occurred north of Dayton near the Cridersville exit in Allen County.

According to the Ohio State Highway Patrol (OSHP), the incident took place around 8:15 p.m. on Northbound I-75. Investigators report that a 2017 Great Dane semi-truck, driven by a 53-year-old man from Florida, failed to maintain an assured clear distance as traffic slowed, triggering a devastating chain-reaction collision.

The victims, identified by the OSHP as Hurshel Gipson, 52, and his 11-year-old son, were passengers in a 2015 Buick Enclave. Both father and son were pronounced dead at the scene. The driver of the Buick, Amanda Gipson, 45, was transported to an area hospital with serious injuries.

Four other individuals were injured in the crash. The driver of the striking semi-truck and a 58-year-old man driving a Ford F-150 were both taken to the hospital with non-life-threatening injuries. Two others in separate vehicles were also hurt.

The section of I-75 Northbound was closed for approximately five hours while crews worked to clear the wreckage and state troopers investigated. The crash remains under active investigation by the Lima and Wapakoneta Posts of the Highway Patrol.

This tragedy marks a particularly difficult weekend for the Southwest Ohio region. Local authorities and news outlets are also simultaneously responding to a mass shooting at a Cincinnati music venue and another fatal crash in Montgomery County involving a juvenile driver. The cumulative impact of these events has cast a somber mood across the Miami Valley.
imageCaption: Aftermath of deadly multi-vehicle crash on Interstate 75 in Allen County, Ohio, which occurred late Saturday night, Feb 28, 2026.

title: Violence Near Clayton Walmart: 18-Year-Old Northmont Student Targeted in Premeditated Attack
description: An 18-year-old Northmont student was shot and pepper-sprayed in a targeted "setup" robbery behind the Clayton Walmart on Feb 28. Clayton Police are investigating a potential school connection as the suspect remains at large. Read the latest on the Virginia Springs Drive investigation.
content:
A quiet residential street in Clayton became the scene of a violent confrontation early Saturday morning, February 28, 2026, when an 18-year-old was shot and pepper-sprayed in what investigators are describing as a targeted "setup." The incident, which occurred in the 5200 block of Virginia Springs Drive, has sent ripples through the Northmont community as details emerge suggesting the victim and the assailant may have shared more than just a zip code.

Midnight Ambush on Virginia Springs Drive

The silence of the suburban neighborhood—located directly behind the Clayton Walmart property line—was shattered around 1:30 a.m. when Montgomery County Regional Dispatch received a frantic 911 call. The caller, an 18-year-old male, reported that he had been shot in the ribs.

First responders arriving on the scene found the victim conscious but in significant distress. In a detail that underscores the premeditated nature of the attack, the victim informed officers that he was first incapacitated with pepper spray before the suspect discharged a firearm. This dual-weapon tactic is a hallmark of coordinated robberies, designed to disorient a victim and prevent them from pursuing or identifying a getaway vehicle.

The Northmont Connection

While initial reports focused on the proximity to the Hoke Road Walmart, the investigation took a more personal turn as the victim provided statements to the Clayton Police. Sources close to the community indicate that the victim recognized his attacker, identifying him as a fellow student or recent acquaintance from Northmont High School.

This revelation shifts the narrative from a random act of suburban violence to a calculated betrayal. Investigators are now pouring over digital evidence, including social media messages and phone records, to determine how the meeting on Virginia Springs Drive was orchestrated. It appears the victim was lured to the residential "blind spot" under the guise of a familiar face, only to be met with a violent ambush.

A Growing Trend of "Setup" Crimes

The Clayton shooting reflects a disturbing trend in the Dayton area involving "meet-up" robberies. Unlike random muggings, these incidents involve a degree of social engineering.

The use of pepper spray suggests the suspect intended to flee quickly with stolen property or cash - and the choice of location, a residential street near a high-traffic commercial zone, allowed the suspect to vanish into the Walmart parking lot or toward Hoke Road within seconds of the shots being fired. Currently, doorbell camera footage from residents on Virginia Springs Drive is being canvassed by the Montgomery County Sheriff’s Office to track the suspect’s movements and identify any fleeing vehicles.

Community Impact and Safety Warnings

The victim was transported to a local hospital for treatment of the gunshot wound to his ribs. While his current condition has not been officially updated, the fact that he was alert enough to provide a suspect description is a critical lead for the Clayton Police Department.

In response to the surge in marketplace and "meet-up" style crimes, law enforcement continues to urge the public to utilize "Safe Exchange Zones." These locations, often found in the parking lots of local police departments like Clayton or Englewood, offer 24/7 surveillance and high visibility - deterrents that are often enough to stop a "setup" in its tracks.

As of Monday afternoon, the shooter remains at large. Police have not yet released the name of the suspect, likely due to his potential status as a juvenile or young adult within the Northmont school system. For now, the community remains on edge, waiting for an arrest that would confirm whether this violence was indeed brought home by one of their own.
imageCaption: Walmart at Hoke Rd. in Englewood, Ohio.
